news_summary: Scientists have made breakthroughs in understanding gold's cosmic origins, uncovered new insights about the Sun's stellar history, expanded minor planet data streams, and volunteers have identified unusually high solar flare activity.
news_summary_fr: Les scientifiques ont fait des avancées décisives dans la compréhension des origines cosmiques de l'or, ont apporté un nouvel éclairage sur l'histoire stellaire du Soleil, ont enrichi les bases de données sur les astéroïdes, et des bénévoles ont détecté une activité inhabituellement intense des éruptions solaires.
news_summary_es: Los científicos han logrado avances decisivos en la comprensión de los orígenes cósmicos del oro, han descubierto nuevos datos sobre la historia estelar del Sol, han ampliado las fuentes de información sobre los planetas menores y los voluntarios han detectado una actividad inusualmente elevada de erupciones solares.

articles.title: Scientists crack a 20-year nuclear mystery behind the creation of gold
articles.summary: Gold and other heavy elements are born in some of the universe’s most violent events—but scientists still struggle to understand the nuclear steps that create them. Now, nuclear physicists have uncovered three key discoveries about how unstable atomic nuclei decay during the rapid neutron-capture process, the chain reaction responsible for forging elements like gold and platinum.
articles.content:
Gold cannot form until certain unstable atomic nuclei break apart. Exactly how those nuclear transformations unfold has long been difficult to determine. Now, nuclear physicists at the University of Tennessee (UT) report three discoveries in a single study that clarify important parts of this process. Their findings could help researchers build improved models of the stellar events that create heavy elements and better predict the behavior of exotic atomic nuclei.

Heavy elements such as gold and platinum are forged under extraordinary conditions, including when stars collapse, explode, or collide. These events trigger the rapid neutron capture process (or r-process for short). During this process, an atomic nucleus absorbs neutrons in rapid succession. As the nucleus grows heavier and more unstable, it eventually breaks down into lighter and more stable forms.

Along this pathway across the nuclide chart, a common sequence involves beta decay of the parent nucleus followed by the release of two neutrons. The atomic nuclei involved in these reactions are extremely rare and unstable, making them difficult or even impossible to study directly in experiments. Because of this, scientists rely heavily on theoretical models, which must be tested and refined using laboratory data.

Studying Rare Nuclei With CERN's ISOLDE Facility

To investigate the process more closely, UT researchers collaborated with scientists from several institutions. The team included UT Graduate Students Peter Dyszel and Jacob Gouge, Professor Robert Grzywacz, Associate Professor Miguel Madurga, and Research Associate Monika Piersa-Silkowska. Their work also built on data analysis methods developed by Research Assistant Professor Zhengyu Xu.

The researchers began with large quantities of the rare isotope indium-134.

"These nuclei are hard to make and require a lot of new technology to synthesize in sufficient quantities," Grzywacz explained.

The team carried out the experiments at the ISOLDE Decay Station at CERN, which produced abundant indium-134 nuclei and used advanced laser separation techniques to ensure their purity. When indium-134 undergoes decay, it generates excited forms of tin-134, tin-133, and tin-132.

Using a neutron detector funded through the National Science Foundation Major Research Instrumentation program and constructed at UT, the scientists uncovered three major findings. The most significant result was the first measurement of neutron energies associated with beta-delayed two-neutron emission.

"The two-neutron emission is the biggest deal," Grzywacz said.

Beta-delayed two-neutron emission occurs only in exotic nuclei, which are unstable and exist only briefly. The energy needed to separate two neutrons from the nucleus is extremely small, but in this experiment it was large enough to measure.

"The reason this is hard is because neutrons like to bounce around. It's hard to tell if it's one or two," Grzywacz explained. In earlier attempts, "no one measured energies," so this approach "opens a completely new field."

This research marks the first detailed study of two-neutron emission from a nucleus that lies along the r-process pathway. The results provide valuable insight for improving models that describe how stellar events create heavy elements such as gold.

A Long-Sought Neutron State in Tin

The team's second major discovery was the first observation of a long predicted single particle neutron state in tin-133. According to Grzywacz, the nucleus begins in an excited state and must release energy to stabilize.

"Tin is in an excited state. (It) has to cool off. It can spit out a neutron, or, with enough energy, it can spit out two neutrons. It should always spit two neutrons, but it doesn't."

Traditionally, scientists believed the tin nucleus simply released neutrons to cool down, effectively losing any trace of the earlier beta decay event. In that scenario the nucleus behaves like an "amnesiac nucleus," with no memory of how it was formed.

"We say the tin doesn't forget," Grzywacz said. "This 'shadow' of indium doesn't completely disappear. The memory is not erased."

Advanced neutron detectors allowed researchers to detect this elusive nuclear state. The observation suggests that current theoretical explanations are incomplete and that scientists need a more sophisticated framework to explain why some decays release one neutron while others release two.

"People were searching for it for 20 years and we found it," Grzywacz said. "Those two neutrons allowed us to see this state."

He noted that the newly observed state represents an intermediate stage in the two-neutron emission sequence. It also represents the final elementary excitation of the tin-133 nucleus, helping complete the nuclear structure picture and improving the accuracy of theoretical calculations.

A Third Discovery Challenges Existing Models

The study also revealed a third important result. Researchers observed a non statistical population of this newly identified state. In simple terms, the way the state is populated during decay does not follow the patterns that scientists typically expect.

Grzywacz explained that the decay environment in this experiment is relatively clean. The nuclear states are separated rather than crowded together.

"You're not making split-pea soup," he said. "Still, in most cases it behaves like split-pea soup. Somehow this statistical mechanism happens. Why is it statistical, even though it shouldn't be and why in our cast it isn't"?

The findings suggest that as scientists explore regions of the nuclear landscape farther from stability, particularly among exotic nuclei such as Tennessine, existing models may no longer apply. New theoretical approaches will likely be required to describe these extreme systems.

The Curiosity Driving New Discoveries

The search for improved models of nuclear structure and element formation offers major opportunities for early career scientists such as Dyszel. He joined Grzywacz's research group in 2022 and served as the first author of the Physical Review Letters paper describing the discoveries.

His responsibilities during the experiment were extensive. Dyszel built frames for neutron tracking detectors and assembled them within the experimental apparatus. He installed electronic systems, constructed beta detectors, performed test measurements, helped develop data acquisition software, adjusted timing systems, and analyzed the resulting data. Despite his broad role, the project remained a collaborative effort involving many researchers.

"The success of this work is due in part to my colleagues and collaborators, whose guidance and constructive input were crucial," he said.

Originally from Jacksonville, Florida, Dyszel joined UT after earning a bachelor's degree in physics from the University of North Florida. His interest in nuclear science began earlier during a general chemistry course, when he first learned about beta decay. The idea that nuclear transformations could create entirely new elements with different properties captured his attention, initially leading him to consider a degree in chemistry.

"It was not until I started my bachelor's degree that I had stepped foot into a physics class, which instantaneously drove me towards a degree in physics," he explained. "I've always been interested in understanding how the world works, and physics has been, and continues to be, the path I want to follow in pursuit of that curiosity."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in understanding the nuclear processes that create heavy elements like gold during stellar events. This discovery has broad implications for astrophysics and nuclear physics, improving models of element formation in the universe, which is inspiring and has meaningful scientific impact beyond a niche audience.
articles.personality_title: Scientists uncover key steps in how gold forms inside stars
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Heavy elements like gold and platinum are created during powerful events in space, such as star explosions or collisions. These elements form through a process called the rapid neutron capture process, or r-process, where atomic nuclei absorb neutrons quickly and then break down into more stable forms. However, the exact nuclear steps in this process have been hard to understand.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Tennessee worked with scientists at CERN to study unstable atomic nuclei involved in forming heavy elements. Using a rare type of indium called indium-134, they performed experiments at CERN’s ISOLDE facility. They made three important discoveries: the first measurement of energies when two neutrons are emitted after beta decay, the detection of a special neutron state in tin-133 that had been predicted but never seen, and a surprising way this neutron state is formed that challenges existing scientific models.

**Impact** – These findings help scientists better understand the nuclear reactions that create elements like gold in stars. For example, discovering the two-neutron emission energy provides new details about how unstable nuclei behave. Seeing the special neutron state in tin-133 shows that nuclei “remember” how they were formed, which was unexpected. This improves the accuracy of models that predict element formation and may change how scientists study exotic atomic nuclei far from stability.

**What’s next step** – The new results suggest that current theories about nuclear decay need updating, especially for very unstable or exotic nuclei. Scientists will work on building more advanced models to explain these unusual behaviors. This research could also guide future experiments studying other rare isotopes and help improve our knowledge of how the universe creates the elements around us.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New experiments reveal detailed nuclear processes that explain how gold and other heavy elements form during violent cosmic events, improving scientific models of element creation.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques dévoilent les étapes clés de la formation de l’or dans les étoiles
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les éléments lourds comme l’or et le platine se forment lors d’événements puissants dans l’espace, tels que les explosions ou collisions d’étoiles. Ces éléments apparaissent grâce à un processus appelé capture rapide de neutrons, ou processus r, où les noyaux atomiques absorbent rapidement des neutrons puis se décomposent en formes plus stables. Cependant, les étapes précises de ce processus sont difficiles à comprendre.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université du Tennessee ont travaillé avec des scientifiques du CERN pour étudier des noyaux atomiques instables impliqués dans la formation des éléments lourds. En utilisant un isotope rare appelé indium-134, ils ont réalisé des expériences au centre ISOLDE du CERN. Ils ont fait trois découvertes importantes : la première mesure des énergies lors de l’émission de deux neutrons après une désintégration bêta, la détection d’un état neutronique spécial dans l’étain-133 prédit mais jamais observé, et une façon surprenante dont cet état neutronique se forme, remettant en question les modèles scientifiques.

**Impact** – Ces découvertes aident les scientifiques à mieux comprendre les réactions nucléaires qui créent des éléments comme l’or dans les étoiles. Par exemple, mesurer l’énergie de l’émission de deux neutrons donne de nouveaux détails sur le comportement des noyaux instables. Observer l’état neutronique spécial dans l’étain-133 montre que les noyaux « se souviennent » de leur formation, ce qui est inattendu. Cela améliore la précision des modèles prédisant la formation des éléments et pourrait changer l’étude des noyaux atomiques exotiques.

**Prochaines étapes** – Ces résultats indiquent que les théories actuelles sur la désintégration nucléaire doivent être mises à jour, surtout pour les noyaux très instables ou exotiques. Les scientifiques travailleront à créer des modèles plus avancés pour expliquer ces comportements inhabituels. Cette recherche guidera aussi de futures expériences sur d’autres isotopes rares et enrichira notre connaissance de la création des éléments dans l’univers.

**Phrase clé** – De nouvelles expériences révèlent les processus nucléaires détaillés expliquant comment l’or et d’autres éléments lourds se forment lors d’événements cosmiques violents, améliorant les modèles scientifiques de la création des éléments.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren pasos clave en la formación del oro dentro de las estrellas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los elementos pesados como el oro y el platino se crean durante eventos poderosos en el espacio, como explosiones o colisiones de estrellas. Estos elementos se forman a través de un proceso llamado captura rápida de neutrones, o proceso r, donde los núcleos atómicos absorben neutrones rápidamente y luego se descomponen en formas más estables. Sin embargo, los pasos exactos de este proceso han sido difíciles de entender.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Tennessee trabajaron con científicos del CERN para estudiar núcleos atómicos inestables que participan en la formación de elementos pesados. Usando un tipo raro de indio llamado indio-134, realizaron experimentos en la instalación ISOLDE del CERN. Hicieron tres descubrimientos importantes: la primera medición de energías cuando se emiten dos neutrones tras una desintegración beta, la detección de un estado especial de neutrones en estaño-133 que se había predicho pero nunca se había visto, y una forma sorprendente en que se forma este estado de neutrones que desafía los modelos científicos actuales.

**Impacto** – Estos hallazgos ayudan a los científicos a comprender mejor las reacciones nucleares que crean elementos como el oro en las estrellas. Por ejemplo, descubrir la energía de la emisión de dos neutrones da nuevos detalles sobre cómo se comportan los núcleos inestables. Ver el estado especial de neutrones en estaño-133 muestra que los núcleos “recuerdan” cómo se formaron, lo cual fue inesperado. Esto mejora la precisión de los modelos que predicen la formación de elementos y puede cambiar cómo se estudian núcleos atómicos exóticos.

**Próximos pasos** – Los nuevos resultados sugieren que las teorías actuales sobre la desintegración nuclear necesitan actualizarse, especialmente para núcleos muy inestables o exóticos. Los científicos trabajarán en construir modelos más avanzados para explicar estos comportamientos inusuales. Esta investigación también podría guiar futuros experimentos con otros isótopos raros y ayudar a mejorar nuestro conocimiento sobre cómo el universo crea los elementos a nuestro alrededor.

**Frase clave** – Nuevos experimentos revelan procesos nucleares detallados que explican cómo se forman el oro y otros elementos pesados durante eventos cósmicos violentos, mejorando los modelos científicos de la creación de elementos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing, golden atomic nucleus at the center, surrounded by swirling, ethereal streams of tiny neutrons gently separating in pairs, set against a softly illuminated cosmic backdrop symbolizing stellar explosions and collisions, with abstract representations of rare isotopes like indium and tin subtly integrated as delicate, shimmering orbs orbiting the nucleus.

articles.title: Our Sun may have escaped the Milky Way’s center with thousands of twin stars
articles.summary: Scientists have uncovered evidence that our Sun may have traveled across the Milky Way as part of a massive migration of Sun-like stars billions of years ago. The journey may have carried the solar system away from the galaxy’s crowded center into a calmer region where life could eventually emerge.
articles.content:
Astronomers have uncovered signs that our Sun may have taken part in a large-scale movement of similar stars that left the inner regions of the Milky Way about 4 to 6 billion years ago. To investigate this possibility, researchers compiled and analyzed an exceptionally precise catalog of stars using observations from the European Space Agency's Gaia satellite. Their results offer new insights into how the Milky Way evolved, particularly the formation of the rotating bar-shaped structure located at the galaxy's center.

On Earth, archaeology reconstructs the past by studying ancient artifacts and remains. In space, scientists use a similar approach called galactic archaeology to piece together the history of stars and galaxies.

Astronomers know that the Sun formed about 4.6 billion years ago at a location more than 10,000 light years closer to the Milky Way's center than where it sits today. Evidence from stellar chemical compositions supports this idea, yet the explanation has long puzzled researchers. Observations of our galaxy show a massive bar-like structure in the central region that produces what scientists call a "corotation barrier." This gravitational effect makes it difficult for stars to travel far outward from the galactic center.

Studying Solar Twins With Gaia

To investigate how the Sun might have reached its current orbit, a research team led by Assistant Professors Daisuke Taniguchi from Tokyo Metropolitan University and Takuji Tsujimoto from the National Astronomical Observatory of Japan carried out a large study of solar "twins." These stars share nearly the same temperature, surface gravity, and chemical composition as our Sun.

The researchers relied on the Gaia satellite mission, which has collected detailed measurements for about two billion stars and other celestial objects. Using this enormous dataset, they assembled a catalog containing 6,594 solar twins. This sample is roughly 30 times larger than those used in earlier surveys.

Age Distribution Reveals a Shared Migration

With this expanded dataset, the team was able to determine the ages of these stars with unprecedented accuracy. They also corrected for selection bias that favors brighter stars that are easier for telescopes to detect.

When the researchers examined the ages of the solar twins, they found a clear concentration of stars between 4 and 6 billion years old. The Sun falls within this same age range. Many of these stars also appear to occupy similar distances from the galactic center. Together, these clues suggest that the Sun's present location is not simply coincidental. Instead, it likely arrived here as part of a much larger outward movement of stars.

Clues to the Formation of the Milky Way's Central Bar

The findings provide new information about the Milky Way's structure and history. Under normal circumstances, the corotation barrier produced by the galaxy's central bar would prevent such a large number of stars from moving away from the inner region. However, the situation could have been different if the bar structure was still forming during that period.

The ages of the solar twins not only point to when this large migration may have happened, but also suggest the time span during which the galactic bar developed.

Why the Sun's Journey Matters for Life

The inner parts of the Milky Way are far more hostile than its outer regions. Conditions near the galactic center include stronger radiation and more frequent interactions between stars. According to the researchers, the Sun's movement away from this crowded environment may have helped place our solar system in a calmer part of the galaxy.

This quieter region provided conditions that allowed life on Earth to eventually emerge and evolve.

This work made use of data products from the European Space Agency (ESA) space mission Gaia and the Two Micron All Sky Survey. It was supported by the Tokyo Center For Excellence Project, Tokyo Metropolitan University, JSPS KAKENHI Grant Numbers 23KJ2149 and 23H00132, the European Union's Horizon 2020 Research and Innovation Program under SPACE-H2020 Grant Agreement Number 101004214 (EXPLORE project).
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about the Sun's migration within the Milky Way, based on extensive data from the Gaia satellite. This finding enhances understanding of galactic evolution and the conditions that allowed life to emerge on Earth, representing a meaningful advancement in space science with broad public interest.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find Sun moved from Milky Way’s center with thousands of similar stars
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long known the Sun formed closer to the center of our galaxy, the Milky Way, about 4.6 billion years ago. But the galaxy’s center is a crowded, dangerous place, and it was unclear how the Sun ended up in its current calmer spot far away.

**What happened** – A team of astronomers used data from the European Space Agency’s Gaia satellite to study over 6,500 “solar twins” – stars very similar to the Sun. They found many of these stars, including the Sun, are between 4 and 6 billion years old and appear to have moved outward from the galaxy’s center together in a large migration.

**Impact** – This discovery helps explain how the Sun escaped the Milky Way’s busy center, where strong radiation and crowded stars could be harmful. Moving away to a quieter region likely helped create safer conditions for life to develop on Earth. It also gives new clues about when and how the galaxy’s central bar-shaped structure formed, which normally blocks stars from moving outward.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will continue studying solar twins and the Milky Way’s structure to better understand how this migration happened and how the galaxy evolved. Future observations may clarify the role of the galactic bar’s formation in allowing stars like the Sun to move outward.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have found that the Sun traveled with thousands of similar stars from the Milky Way’s center to a safer region, helping set the stage for life on Earth.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent que le Soleil a quitté le centre de la Voie lactée avec des milliers d’étoiles semblables
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques savent depuis longtemps que le Soleil s’est formé plus près du centre de notre galaxie, la Voie lactée, il y a environ 4,6 milliards d’années. Mais le centre de la galaxie est un endroit très dense et dangereux, et on ne comprenait pas comment le Soleil s’est retrouvé dans sa position plus calme actuelle.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe d’astronomes a utilisé les données du satellite Gaia de l’Agence spatiale européenne pour étudier plus de 6 500 « jumeaux solaires » – des étoiles très similaires au Soleil. Ils ont découvert que beaucoup de ces étoiles, y compris le Soleil, ont entre 4 et 6 milliards d’années et semblent avoir migré ensemble du centre vers l’extérieur de la galaxie.

**Impact** – Cette découverte explique comment le Soleil a échappé au centre très actif de la Voie lactée, où les radiations fortes et la densité d’étoiles pouvaient être dangereuses. En s’éloignant vers une région plus calme, il a probablement créé des conditions plus sûres pour que la vie apparaisse sur Terre. Cela donne aussi des indices sur la formation de la structure centrale en forme de barre de la galaxie, qui bloque normalement le déplacement des étoiles vers l’extérieur.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à étudier les jumeaux solaires et la structure de la Voie lactée pour mieux comprendre cette migration et l’évolution de la galaxie. De futures observations pourraient préciser le rôle de la formation de la barre galactique dans ce déplacement.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les scientifiques ont découvert que le Soleil a voyagé avec des milliers d’étoiles semblables du centre de la Voie lactée vers une région plus sûre, ce qui a aidé à créer les conditions nécessaires à la vie sur Terre.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren que el Sol se desplazó desde el centro de la Vía Láctea con miles de estrellas similares
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos saben desde hace tiempo que el Sol se formó más cerca del centro de nuestra galaxia, la Vía Láctea, hace unos 4.6 mil millones de años. Pero el centro de la galaxia es un lugar muy denso y peligroso, y no se entendía cómo el Sol llegó a su posición actual más tranquila.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de astrónomos usó datos del satélite Gaia de la Agencia Espacial Europea para estudiar más de 6,500 “gemelos solares” – estrellas muy parecidas al Sol. Encontraron que muchas de estas estrellas, incluido el Sol, tienen entre 4 y 6 mil millones de años y parecen haberse movido juntas desde el centro hacia afuera de la galaxia.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento ayuda a explicar cómo el Sol escapó del centro activo de la Vía Láctea, donde la radiación fuerte y la densidad de estrellas podían ser peligrosas. Al moverse hacia una región más tranquila, probablemente permitió condiciones más seguras para que la vida se desarrollara en la Tierra. También aporta pistas sobre cuándo y cómo se formó la estructura en forma de barra del centro galáctico, que normalmente impide que las estrellas se alejen.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán estudiando a los gemelos solares y la estructura de la Vía Láctea para entender mejor esta migración y la evolución de la galaxia. Observaciones futuras podrían aclarar el papel de la formación de la barra galáctica en este movimiento.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los científicos han descubierto que el Sol viajó con miles de estrellas similares desde el centro de la Vía Láctea hacia una región más segura, ayudando a crear las condiciones para la vida en la Tierra.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a radiant golden sun surrounded by a swirling cluster of nearly identical smaller stars, all gently drifting outward from a glowing, abstract bar-shaped structure representing the Milky Way’s center, set against a soft cosmic backdrop of muted blues and purples.

articles.title: Extra Extra! Extra Data Stream Added to the Daily Minor Planet!
articles.summary: The Daily Minor Planet citizen science project is expanding! In addition to data received nightly from the Catalina Sky Survey’s Mt. Lemmon telescope in Arizona, the project’s science team is now processing images from the Bok 2.3-meter telescope at Kitt Peak National Observatory. The Bok is a mighty telescope run by the University of Arizona’s […]
articles.content:
The Daily Minor Planet citizen science project is expanding! In addition to data received nightly from the Catalina Sky Survey's Mt. Lemmon telescope in Arizona, the project’s science team is now processing images from the Bok 2.3-meter telescope at Kitt Peak National Observatory. The Bok is a mighty telescope run by the University of Arizona's Steward Observatory that is used to survey for new near-Earth objects (NEOs) – asteroids that cross Earth’s orbit.

Data from the Bok telescope peers deeper than the data from the Mt. Lemmon telescope–it reveals objects roughly two to three times as faint. Software often struggles with such faint objects, but humans shine at pattern recognition in this kind of data, making your contributions to this search more valuable than ever.

Another important feature of the new data is that it mostly comes from the ecliptic, the band of sky where asteroids and comets preferentially travel. The project team expects this deeper, ecliptic-focused coverage to substantially increase the number of main-belt asteroids they can recover and confirm and bring fresh waves of near-Earth asteroid candidates.

Keep an eye out for new Bok subject sets as they are added. They'll be a little more challenging and a lot more rewarding!

The Daily Minor Planet is a regularly updated citizen science project hosted by the Zooniverse using nightly data collected by the Catalina Sky Survey. Anyone with a laptop or smartphone can join.
articles.reasoning: The article describes an expansion of a citizen science project that enhances the detection and tracking of near-Earth objects using deeper and more focused telescope data. This has broad significance for planetary defense and scientific discovery, involves public participation, and provides detailed context about the improvement and its expected impact.
articles.personality_title: Daily Minor Planet project adds new telescope data to find more asteroids
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Daily Minor Planet is a citizen science project where people help find asteroids by looking at telescope images. Until now, it used data from the Mt. Lemmon telescope in Arizona, which scans the sky every night.

**What happened** – The project has started using images from the Bok telescope at Kitt Peak National Observatory, also in Arizona. This telescope is stronger and can see much fainter objects in space, especially near the path where most asteroids travel.

**Impact** – Adding the Bok telescope data means volunteers can help discover many more asteroids, including those closer to Earth. The new images are harder to analyze but can reveal objects two to three times fainter than before. Since people are good at spotting patterns, their help is very important.

**What’s next step** – The project will keep adding new sets of images from the Bok telescope for volunteers to study. This will likely lead to finding new main-belt asteroids and near-Earth objects that need to be tracked.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The Daily Minor Planet project now uses deeper sky images from a second telescope, letting volunteers find more and fainter asteroids than ever before.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le projet Daily Minor Planet ajoute des données d’un nouveau télescope pour découvrir plus d’astéroïdes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Daily Minor Planet est un projet de science participative où les gens aident à trouver des astéroïdes en regardant des images de télescopes. Jusqu’à présent, il utilisait les données du télescope Mt. Lemmon en Arizona, qui observe le ciel chaque nuit.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le projet utilise maintenant des images du télescope Bok à l’Observatoire national de Kitt Peak, aussi en Arizona. Ce télescope est plus puissant et peut voir des objets beaucoup plus faibles dans l’espace, surtout près de la trajectoire où se déplacent la plupart des astéroïdes.

**Impact** – L’ajout des données du télescope Bok permet aux volontaires de découvrir beaucoup plus d’astéroïdes, y compris ceux proches de la Terre. Les nouvelles images sont plus difficiles à analyser mais peuvent révéler des objets deux à trois fois plus faibles qu’avant. Comme les humains sont bons pour repérer des motifs, leur aide est très importante.

**Prochaine étape** – Le projet continuera à ajouter de nouveaux ensembles d’images du télescope Bok pour que les volontaires les étudient. Cela devrait permettre de trouver de nouveaux astéroïdes de la ceinture principale et des objets proches de la Terre à suivre.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le projet Daily Minor Planet utilise désormais des images plus profondes d’un second télescope, permettant aux volontaires de trouver plus d’astéroïdes, et plus faibles, que jamais.
articles.personality_title_es: El proyecto Daily Minor Planet añade datos de un nuevo telescopio para encontrar más asteroides
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Daily Minor Planet es un proyecto de ciencia ciudadana donde las personas ayudan a encontrar asteroides mirando imágenes de telescopios. Hasta ahora, usaba datos del telescopio Mt. Lemmon en Arizona, que observa el cielo cada noche.

**Qué pasó** – El proyecto comenzó a usar imágenes del telescopio Bok en el Observatorio Nacional Kitt Peak, también en Arizona. Este telescopio es más potente y puede ver objetos mucho más tenues en el espacio, especialmente cerca de la ruta donde viajan la mayoría de los asteroides.

**Impacto** – Añadir datos del telescopio Bok significa que los voluntarios pueden ayudar a descubrir muchos más asteroides, incluyendo los cercanos a la Tierra. Las nuevas imágenes son más difíciles de analizar, pero pueden mostrar objetos dos o tres veces más tenues que antes. Como los humanos son buenos para reconocer patrones, su ayuda es muy valiosa.

**Próximo paso** – El proyecto seguirá añadiendo nuevos conjuntos de imágenes del telescopio Bok para que los voluntarios las estudien. Esto probablemente llevará a encontrar nuevos asteroides del cinturón principal y objetos cercanos a la Tierra para monitorear.

**Mensaje en una frase** – El proyecto Daily Minor Planet ahora usa imágenes más profundas de un segundo telescopio, permitiendo a los voluntarios encontrar más asteroides y más tenues que nunca.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of two large telescopes—one labeled Mt. Lemmon and the other Bok—set against a twilight sky filled with faint, glowing asteroid trails concentrated along a bright, curved band representing the ecliptic, with subtle human silhouettes gazing upward, symbolizing citizen scientists contributing to deep-space discovery.

articles.title: Volunteers Find Oddly High Solar Flare Rates
articles.summary: Patches of the Sun’s surface often show strong magnetic fields. These fields can emerge within a matter of hours, and can decay slowly or quickly, sometimes over days, weeks, or even months. Thanks to a new study about these long-lived active regions, we now know much more about the patches where these strong magnetic fields […]
articles.content:
Patches of the Sun’s surface often show strong magnetic fields. These fields can emerge within a matter of hours, and can decay slowly or quickly, sometimes over days, weeks, or even months. Thanks to a new study about these long-lived active regions, we now know much more about the patches where these strong magnetic fields take at least a month to decay.

This study relied on inputs from NASA’s Solar Active Region Spotter citizen science project, which asked volunteers to answer a series of questions about pairs of active region images from NASA’s Solar Dynamics Observatory.

Project leads Emily Mason (Predictive Science Inc.) and Kara Kniezewski (Air Force Institute of Technology) looked at the data and the analysis done by volunteers. They found that the long-lived active regions produce disproportionately more flares than the shorter-lived regions and are 3-6 times more likely than other active regions to be the source of the most intense kinds of solar flares. These results are a strong indication that long-lived active regions are crucial for predicting space weather and could provide critical information on magnetic fields deeper inside the Sun.

The Solar Active Region Spotter project is now complete, but you can learn more about the results here: https://www.zooniverse.org/projects/eimason/solar-active-region-spotter/about/results

Explore NASA Citizen Science projects you can join today to help advance our understanding of space weather: https://go.nasa.gov/3ZK6nvE.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant scientific discovery about long-lived solar active regions and their role in producing intense solar flares, which has broad implications for predicting space weather. This research involved citizen science, engaging the public in meaningful scientific contribution, and advances understanding of solar magnetic fields with potential large-scale benefits for space weather forecasting and related technological protections on Earth.
articles.personality_title: Volunteers Help Reveal Why Some Solar Flares Are Stronger and Last Longer
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Sun's surface has areas called active regions where magnetic fields are very strong. These magnetic fields can appear quickly and then fade away at different speeds, sometimes lasting from days to months.

**What happened** – A new study used help from volunteers in NASA’s Solar Active Region Spotter project. These volunteers looked at pictures of the Sun taken by NASA’s Solar Dynamics Observatory and answered questions about pairs of active regions. Scientists Emily Mason and Kara Kniezewski analyzed this information and found that active regions lasting more than a month produce many more solar flares, especially the strongest ones.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that long-lasting active regions are very important for predicting solar flares, which can affect space weather and technology on Earth. Knowing which regions are more likely to cause big solar flares helps scientists better understand the Sun’s magnetic fields and improve forecasts that protect satellites and power systems.

**What's next step** – Although the Solar Active Region Spotter project has finished, scientists will use the new knowledge to improve space weather predictions. People interested in space can also join other NASA citizen science projects to help study the Sun and space weather.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Volunteers helped scientists find that long-lasting magnetic regions on the Sun are key sources of strong solar flares, improving our ability to predict space weather.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des bénévoles aident à comprendre pourquoi certaines éruptions solaires sont plus fortes et durent plus longtemps
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La surface du Soleil présente des zones appelées régions actives où les champs magnétiques sont très forts. Ces champs peuvent apparaître rapidement puis disparaître à des vitesses différentes, parfois en quelques jours à plusieurs mois.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une nouvelle étude a utilisé l’aide de bénévoles dans le cadre du projet Solar Active Region Spotter de la NASA. Ces volontaires ont examiné des photos du Soleil prises par l’Observatoire de la dynamique solaire de la NASA et répondu à des questions sur des paires de régions actives. Les scientifiques Emily Mason et Kara Kniezewski ont analysé ces données et découvert que les régions actives qui durent plus d’un mois produisent beaucoup plus d’éruptions solaires, surtout les plus intenses.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que les régions actives de longue durée sont très importantes pour prévoir les éruptions solaires, qui peuvent influencer le climat spatial et la technologie sur Terre. Savoir quelles régions sont plus susceptibles de provoquer de fortes éruptions aide les scientifiques à mieux comprendre les champs magnétiques du Soleil et à améliorer les prévisions pour protéger les satellites et les réseaux électriques.

**Prochaine étape** – Bien que le projet Solar Active Region Spotter soit terminé, les scientifiques utiliseront ces nouvelles connaissances pour mieux prévoir le climat spatial. Les personnes intéressées peuvent aussi rejoindre d’autres projets de science participative de la NASA pour aider à étudier le Soleil et le climat spatial.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des bénévoles ont aidé les scientifiques à découvrir que les régions magnétiques durables du Soleil sont des sources clés des fortes éruptions solaires, améliorant ainsi notre capacité à prévoir le climat spatial.
articles.personality_title_es: Voluntarios ayudan a descubrir por qué algunas erupciones solares son más fuertes y duran más
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La superficie del Sol tiene zonas llamadas regiones activas donde los campos magnéticos son muy fuertes. Estos campos pueden aparecer rápidamente y desaparecer a diferentes velocidades, a veces en días o incluso meses.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo estudio usó la ayuda de voluntarios en el proyecto Solar Active Region Spotter de la NASA. Estos voluntarios observaron imágenes del Sol tomadas por el Observatorio de Dinámica Solar de la NASA y respondieron preguntas sobre pares de regiones activas. Las científicas Emily Mason y Kara Kniezewski analizaron esta información y encontraron que las regiones activas que duran más de un mes producen muchas más erupciones solares, especialmente las más fuertes.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra que las regiones activas de larga duración son muy importantes para predecir las erupciones solares, que pueden afectar el clima espacial y la tecnología en la Tierra. Saber qué regiones tienen más probabilidad de causar grandes erupciones ayuda a los científicos a entender mejor los campos magnéticos del Sol y a mejorar los pronósticos para proteger satélites y sistemas eléctricos.

**Próximo paso** – Aunque el proyecto Solar Active Region Spotter ha terminado, los científicos usarán este nuevo conocimiento para mejorar las predicciones del clima espacial. Las personas interesadas también pueden unirse a otros proyectos de ciencia ciudadana de la NASA para ayudar a estudiar el Sol y el clima espacial.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los voluntarios ayudaron a los científicos a descubrir que las regiones magnéticas duraderas en el Sol son fuentes clave de fuertes erupciones solares, mejorando nuestra capacidad para predecir el clima espacial.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing, textured sun surface with vivid swirling patches of bright magnetic fields that resemble fiery, intertwined ribbons lasting prominently over time, with subtle radiant bursts symbolizing intense solar flares emerging from these long-lived regions against a simple, soft gradient background.
